steps.ordered: CT Abdomen (CT ABD & PELVIS W/O CONTRAST)

ensemble.other_hypothesis: Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) — given family history of 'stomach aneurysm' in oldest brother, extensive PVD, CAD, hypertension, and RLQ pain radiating to the back with hemodynamic instability (low BP at home); also consider renal/urologic pathology given CKD and back radiation, or mesenteric ischemia given PVD
ensemble.information_gap: Whether there is a structural vascular or solid-organ abdominal pathology (most critically an AAA or aortic process) accounting for RLQ pain radiating to the back, hemodynamic compromise, and leukocytosis in a patient with known PVD and a family history of aortic aneurysm
ensemble.expected_finding: Aortic aneurysm with or without periaortic stranding/hematoma, or alternatively inflammatory/infectious process (appendicitis, diverticulitis, pyelonephritis/perinephric abscess) or bowel pathology explaining the pain and leukocytosis
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The combination of back-radiating RLQ pain, hemodynamic instability, PVD, leukocytosis, and a first-degree family history of 'stomach aneurysm' makes CT abdomen/pelvis the appropriate next step to rapidly survey for vascular, inflammatory, and structural pathology; contrast is withheld given stage 4 CKD (eGFR ~19).
grounding: History: 'RLQ abdominal discomfort that radiates into his back for the last ___ days'
History: 'blood pressure has been low at home and they have been holding his BP meds'
History: 'Oldest brother died from stomach aneurysm'
PMH: 'PVD, CAD s/p 3 vessel CABG, HTN, IDDM'
Labs: 'Creatinine: 3.1 mg/dL', 'Estimated GFR = 19 if non African-American'
Labs: 'Troponin T: <0.01' — acute MI excluded as primary cause
Prior imaging: Chest X-ray — no acute cardiopulmonary process identified
ensemble.reasoning: This patient has RLQ pain radiating to the back with hemodynamic instability in the setting of known PVD, extensive atherosclerotic disease, and a family history of aortic aneurysm ('stomach aneurysm' in brother) — the clinical picture is highly concerning for AAA or another vascular catastrophe that must be excluded urgently. Pancreatitis and cardiac causes have been effectively ruled out by labs, and the chest X-ray is unremarkable, so CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast (chosen to protect his severely reduced renal function with eGFR ~19) is the logical next step to survey the aorta, retroperitoneum, and abdominal viscera simultaneously.

representative_ex_ante.other_hypothesis: Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) — given family history of 'stomach aneurysm' in oldest brother, extensive PVD, CAD, hypertension, and RLQ pain radiating to the back with hemodynamic instability (low BP at home); also consider renal/urologic pathology given CKD and back radiation, or mesenteric ischemia given PVD

representative_ex_ante.expected_finding: Aortic aneurysm with or without periaortic stranding/hematoma, or alternatively inflammatory/infectious process (appendicitis, diverticulitis, pyelonephritis/perinephric abscess) or bowel pathology explaining the pain and leukocytosis

History: 'blood pressure has been low at home and they have been holding his BP meds'
History: 'Oldest brother died from stomach aneurysm'

representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This patient has RLQ pain radiating to the back with hemodynamic instability in the setting of known PVD, extensive atherosclerotic disease, and a family history of aortic aneurysm ('stomach aneurysm' in brother) — the clinical picture is highly concerning for AAA or another vascular catastrophe that must be excluded urgently. Pancreatitis and cardiac causes have been effectively ruled out by labs, and the chest X-ray is unremarkable, so CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast (chosen to protect his severely reduced renal function with eGFR ~19) is the logical next step to survey the aorta, retroperitoneum, and abdominal viscera simultaneously.

vindication.actual_finding: CT demonstrated severe right lower quadrant inflammation centered on a dilated (12 mm) appendix with adjacent extraluminal gas locules consistent with perforated or near-perforated appendicitis, and the infrarenal aorta was only mildly dilated at 3.1 cm without aneurysm or periaortic pathology.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The scan resolved the information gap by identifying an inflammatory/infectious abdominal process (appendicitis) as one of the explicitly anticipated alternative explanations for RLQ pain and leukocytosis, while ruling out a clinically significant aortic aneurysm or aortic process.
